nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral-rich Red Sea reefs from about 5 to 45 m depth, most commonly below 25 m, where small aggregations form above coral and rubble zones.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Red Sea coral reef; mid- to deeper reef zones with mixed coral and rubble where small aggregations form above the substrate.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs for retreat

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied carnivore diet of vitamin-enriched frozen mysis, frozen brine shrimp and other finely chopped meaty seafoods alongside a quality marine flake/pellet; small frequent feedings (2-3 times daily) tend to maintain coloration and condition.

avoidWith: triggers and large angelfish that may bully them
predatory fish that could eat a small wrasse
multiple males of the same species in a single tank

imageCandidates.notes: Julien Renoult; 2048x1365. Tack-sharp in-situ Red Sea male in full nuptial display - vivid orange/yellow body, blue longitudinal lines, bright red dorsal and anal fins, red eye; whole fish framed.

Julien Renoult; 2048x1365. Sharp initial/female-phase individual with a second fish behind it, showing the harem/social context; correct ID with lavender longitudinal lines but less vibrant than the display male.

summary: The Eightline Flasher Wrasse (Paracheilinus octotaenia) is a small, peaceful Red Sea reef fish named for the eight dark horizontal lines along its body. Males display vivid orange, yellow, and red courtship colors. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, though a tight-fitting lid is essential — flasher wrasses are well-known jumpers.
careNotes:
The Eightline Flasher Wrasse is a small reef fish, reaching around 8–10 cm, endemic to the Red Sea and the adjacent western Indian Ocean. Like other members of the genus Paracheilinus, it is generally regarded as beginner-friendly — peaceful toward tankmates, widely considered reef-safe, and tending to accept prepared marine foods without difficulty.

The species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life female and some transition to male as they mature. In the wild it lives in harems, and aquarists typically keep one male alongside two to five females. This arrangement encourages the male to perform his characteristic 'flashing' display, during which his coloration intensifies dramatically into vivid yellow, orange, and red highlights over the eight dark body stripes. When keeping a group, introduce the females simultaneously or ahead of the male, and never house more than one male per tank — males will fight.

A tight-fitting lid or mesh top is essential. Flasher wrasses are well-known jumpers and have been reported lost from open-topped reef tanks. A minimum tank of around 250 L provides enough open mid-water swimming space for a small harem with room for retreat.

A note on names: the Eightline Flasher Wrasse is sometimes confused with Pseudocheilinus octotaenia, a species known in the trade as the Eightline Wrasse. Despite sharing a species epithet, these are distinct genera with different care requirements and temperaments.
breedingNotes: The Eightline Flasher Wrasse is egg-scattering (oviparous) and breeds with distinct male–female pairing. Males perform elaborate twilight courtship displays in which their fin and body coloration intensifies dramatically. Captive spawning is occasionally reported, but rearing the small, free-floating (pelagic) larvae is generally considered difficult and is not routinely accomplished in home aquaria.
